questions.prompt: A run produces wiggum_scores r1: {depth: 8, relevance: 7, completeness: 9, grounded: 5, specificity: 6, structure: 8}. What is the weighted r1 score?
questions.explanation: Weighted score = 8×0.25 + 7×0.20 + 9×0.20 + 5×0.15 + 6×0.10 + 8×0.10 = 2.00 + 1.40 + 1.80 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.80 = 7.35. Grounded (5×0.15 = 0.75) is the weakest contributor — a score of 5 means the evaluator found confident claims not supported by the research context. Depth at 8 is solid; the gap is in source fidelity and specificity.
questions.hint: Apply the six weights in order: depth×0.25 + relevance×0.20 + completeness×0.20 + grounded×0.15 + specificity×0.10 + structure×0.10.

questions.prompt: The composite score formula is `0.7 * mean_wiggum_r1 + 0.3 * criteria_rate * 10`. A session of 15 runs has mean_wiggum_r1=7.8 and 12/15 runs pass hard criteria. What is the composite score?
questions.hint: criteria_rate = passing_runs / total_runs. Plug into 0.7 × wiggum_r1 + 0.3 × criteria_rate × 10.

questions.prompt: The Wiggum loop runs with WIGGUM_PASS_THRESHOLD=9.0 and WIGGUM_MAX_ROUNDS=3. Round 1 score: 8.3. Round 2 score: 8.7. Round 3 score: 9.1. What is the outcome?

options.text: PASS — round 3 scores 9.1, meeting the 9.0 threshold
PASS — round 2 scores 8.7, which already meets a typical quality bar
FAIL — no round reached 9.0 within the first two rounds, so max rounds are exhausted
FAIL — the average across three rounds (8.7) is below the 9.0 threshold

questions.explanation: The loop terminates PASS as soon as any round's score meets or exceeds WIGGUM_PASS_THRESHOLD=9.0. Round 3 scores 9.1 ≥ 9.0 → PASS. Note that both 8.3 and 8.7 would have triggered PASS under the earlier 8.0 threshold — the higher bar requires genuinely excellent output, not just above-average. The per-round score determines PASS/FAIL; the average across rounds is irrelevant to termination.
questions.hint: The loop terminates as soon as any single round meets or exceeds the threshold. What is the threshold, and which round first meets it?

questions.prompt: A Wiggum issue string reads: 'Section 2 lacks a specific implementation example; only describes the concept without showing how to apply it.' Which failure class does this belong to?

options.text: Task Completion Drift — the agent didn't produce the output
Encoding Corruption — the section content is malformed
Placeholder Content Generation — the section describes without implementing
Count Constraint Violation — Section 2 should not exist

questions.explanation: This is the most common failure class (45+ occurrences). 'Describes the concept without showing how to apply it' is the definition of placeholder content — the section exists and is on-topic (not task drift), but it describes what to do rather than showing how to do it. The depth dimension catches this.
questions.hint: Which failure class specifically describes content that describes rather than implements?

questions.prompt: Why is the revision prompt designed to give the producer the full issue list (specific problems) rather than a general 'improve this output' instruction?

options.text: Specific issues are required by the Wiggum protocol — it's a formatting constraint, not a pedagogical choice
Specific issues produce targeted revisions rather than wholesale rewrites, reducing token cost and preserving the parts that scored well
General instructions cause the model to hallucinate new content unrelated to the original task
The producer model cannot interpret general feedback — it requires structured JSON input

questions.explanation: Targeted revision is the design goal. The evaluator has already done the work of identifying what is wrong. A general 'improve' prompt causes the model to rewrite sections that scored 9/10 as well as sections that scored 5/10 — wasting tokens and potentially degrading the high-scoring sections. A specific issue list focuses revision on the actual problems.
questions.hint: Think about what 'improve this output' would cause the model to do vs. a specific issue list.

questions.prompt: An orchestrator subtask produces output that overlaps substantially with a sibling subtask. Which harness-side countermeasure most directly addresses this?

options.text: Running Wiggum on each subtask independently before assembly
Adding explicit out-of-scope boundaries to each subtask prompt, telling each agent what the other subtasks cover
Reducing MAX_WORKERS to 1 so subtasks run sequentially and can reference each other
Increasing the novelty threshold to filter out overlapping search results

questions.explanation: Role ambiguity (the failure class this belongs to) is caused by insufficient scope boundaries in the subtask prompts. The countermeasure is explicit out-of-scope declarations: 'The following material is covered by other subtasks and must NOT appear in your output: [...]'. This is more constraining than positive scope descriptions because it directly prohibits the overlapping content.
questions.hint: The failure taxonomy reading describes a specific countermeasure for role ambiguity in orchestration.
